props.covers: The shipped shape contract after the object-definition overhaul: ObjectDef v2 and the two-tier ShapeDef registry, the text-slot preset library, def-declared outlines/anchors/hit-testing, the 10-pick ink/fill/wash color role system, and the end-to-end recipe for adding a shape.

text.insert: in this engine is not a drawing primitive — it is a typed, agent-editable JSON record: a member of the closed
, no rotation anywhere in the schema), a single

pick from a closed 10-pick roster. Everything else about a kind — how it paints, where its text lives and is edited, its connection outline and anchors, its resize handles, its selection toolbar, its creation defaults — is declared on one registered definition object, and every consumer (render, routing, interaction, editor chrome) derives its behavior from that declaration. There is no freeform vector path, no per-instance transform, and no plugin-registered shape class; every mutation flows through the single reducer documented in
This is the shipped result of the object-definition overhaul (
, decisions D1–D19, implemented 2026-07-08): the four legacy color vocabularies collapsed into one stored

field, outline geometry moved out of the routing layer into the defs, and the old ten-artifact add-a-shape checklist shrank to one def file plus a handful of one-line vocabulary entries. This doc specifies the shipped contract, bottom-up: the def types, the text-slot library, the outline/anchor system, the color system, the file layout, and the end-to-end recipe for adding a shape.

Every object stores its text in the single required
field (D3/D11; it may be empty). The kind decides rendering: sections render it as the floating title chip, stickies render simple markdown (D18: H1–H3, bullets, bold, inline code —
), shapes render it in their declared slot. Connections keep their own separate
. Where and how that text renders — and is edited — is a
picked from a small named preset library, never per-shape ad-hoc CSS:
The invariant that fixed editing (D14): RENDERER AND EDITOR CONSUME THE SAME SLOT DESCRIPTOR.
is the one rect/typography source; the at-rest renderer (
) both call it, so at rest and mid-edit are pixel-identical, caret aside — no gray-out, no visually distinct overlay. While an object's text is being edited, the def's renderer receives
so the editor is the only visible copy. Adding a placement to the library automatically makes editing correct for every def that uses it.
Preset assignments in the shipped defs: most shapes take the
default (15px/700, centered, dark — D6's baseline); the icon glyph family takes

functions (text centers in the body excluding head/notch — replacing the old
margin hacks). Pure glyphs — plus, or-junction, summing-junction — declare
is false, so they refuse the editor.
The outline is the geometric primitive (D4): each def declares its connection/hit geometry as an
, and connection anchors, anchor dots, outline snap, and pointer hit-testing all derive from it. It lives in
— a pure, React-free leaf module. The import-boundaries test allows

; geometry.ts is where the connector-relevant outline math lives, but no single edge is pinned. A silhouette is how the shape paints; the outline is where connectors attach and clicks land — the two should trace the same curve, cross-checked by

switch's dispatch order VERBATIM — including its quirks — so connection geometry stayed byte-identical through the P3 move. The quirk is documented in the source and worth quoting exactly, because it is NOT the render dispatch:
Anchors are the 4-ray outline projection, and that is the default for every kind — no def declares anchor points explicitly.
(10 world px) outside the bound, then projects each along the ray from the object's center onto the outline polygon (
), falling back to the bbox edge midpoint only for degenerate geometry. Three consumers read the same four points: the live connect cascade (
— anchor snap within 8 view px, else outline snap within 8 world px, else inside, else free; unchanged from the pre-overhaul cascade, see
), and the selected-object anchor dots below — so the dots you see, the ports you grab, and the points connections snap to can never disagree.
renders FigJam-style dots (8px white fill, selection-blue ring, 14px grab target) in CanvasStage's screen-space overlay. In select mode, dots appear for every SELECTED object, at the
; in Connector Mode, dots are hover-driven for the topmost object under the cursor and use
so they are ungated by zoom. They replace the old invisible edge ports. Select-mode visibility and interactivity are zoom-gated by
(0.5) — below 50% zoom, select-mode dots are hidden and inert at

; Connector Mode bypasses the gate so its dots stay visible and interactive. Resize corners are unaffected: SelectionBox shows at any zoom. A pointerdown on a visible, interactive dot bubbles to the stage's
resolver and starts a connector-create gesture exactly like the old ports.
walks topmost-first, fast-rejects on the bbox, then asks
— bbox-outline kinds test the plain bounds (byte-identical to pre-D16), true-outline kinds test point-in-polygon expanded by
(4 world px) so the stroke itself stays clickable. A diamond's empty corners click through to whatever is behind. Three surfaces are DELIBERATELY still bbox-based, each documented in
— a drag-select rectangle grabbing a diamond's empty corner still selects it, FigJam-consistent), drop targeting (
— sections-only, and sections are bbox anyway), and snap-guide candidates (
— alignment targets are axis-aligned bounds regardless of outline shape).

props.title: Clicks respect the declared outline; drag-select, drop targets, and snapping deliberately stay bbox

text.insert: so true-outline shapes are only clickable where they actually paint, while drag-select, section drop-targeting, and snap-candidate gathering remain bounding-box tests on purpose — they are area/alignment semantics, not point-precision semantics, and FigJam behaves the same way. Bbox-outline kinds are byte-identical to the pre-D16 behavior by construction (their outline IS the bbox and no polygon is ever materialized), which is what made the cutover safe to gate on the existing interaction test suite.

(D1). The vocabulary is FigJam's color popover, minus the custom-color wheel and minus black (D7 + D12): 10 hues, one pick per hue. Each pick resolves through ink/fill/wash roles: ink is the picker swatch and every stroke, fill is the shape/sticky/chip fill, and wash is the section tint. Legacy `-soft` ids were remapped once by tools/migrate-canvas-docs/migrate.ts to the nearest bare hue; at runtime the validator simply drops unknown ids, so there is no live normalization path in isCanvasColor(). The ids are schema vocabulary in
— no hexes there, just the closed id set and its
The design-intent palette sheet (per-hue ink/fill/wash swatches + hex table) lives at

The 10-pick roster in picker order (CANVAS_COLORS — the single row every kind renders):

  gray  red  orange  yellow  green  teal  blue  violet  pink  white

Legacy ids ending in `-soft` were remapped once by tools/migrate-canvas-docs/migrate.ts to the nearest bare hue. At runtime, isCanvasColor drops unknown ids; there is no live normalization path.
What each id LOOKS like per kind lives in
— the canonical 10-row swatch table, a leaf module in theme/ (sibling of

: one picker-preview hex plus one expression cell per role:
The design-intent palette sheet (per-hue ink/fill/wash swatches + hex table) lives at

Authoritative role hexes:

hue     ink      shape fill  sticky fill  section wash
gray    #757575  #E6E6E6    #E6E6E6     #F9F9F9
red     #F24822  #FFC7C2    #FFAFA3     #FFF5F5
orange  #EB7500  #FFE0C2    #FFE0C2     #FFF7F0
yellow  #E8A302  #FFECBD    #FFE299     #FFFBF0
green   #14AE5C  #DDF8E2    #DDF8E2     #EBFFEE
teal    #369E94  #C6FAF6    #C6FAF6     #EAFDFB
blue    #0D99FF  #C2E5FF    #A8DAFF     #F5FBFF
violet  #9747FF  #DCCCFF    #DCCCFF     #F8F5FF
pink    #F849C1  #FFC2EC    #FFC2EC     #FFF0FA
white   #757575  #FFFFFF    #FFFFFF     #FFFFFF

White uses #757575 for strokes/connectors; its picker preview remains #FFFFFF with the existing contrast ring. The white section chip uses #E6E6E6 fill and #C4C4C4 border so it remains visible on white wash.
A pick is a direction; the def's
role, every pick renders fill + ink border at the universal 4px stroke (
); there is no borderless color treatment. White keeps a visible
stroke since a white stroke would vanish on the
, wash = the tint and the title chip uses fill + ink border; for
, fill = the sticky hex; for connectors, ink = the stroke hex (default pick
). The one resolver every object renderer goes through is
, which also fixes text: ALL object text is

dark (D8) (one deviation: the sticky inset-body preset uses rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.8)) — no per-color text derivation and no user-pickable text color. D13 killed forced glyph colors: icon glyphs are ordinary colorable objects.
New-object color is last-picked memory (D17), per kind so a red connector doesn't make the next sticky red: reducer state carries
when no explicit color is supplied, and a
color patch writes it back. The UI is ONE component:
— identical previews for every kind, the current pick marked by a floating 2px #8C2EF2 rounded-square outline (the rainbow conic ring is retired) — replacing the old
, section-tint flyout, and connector swatch row.

props.title: The color roster is closed: 10 picks, no custom colors, ever

text.insert: Documents store swatch IDS, never hexes.
(unknown ids are dropped with a warning); the legacy
/raw connector hexes are deleted from the schema with no legacy read path (D10) — existing documents crossed over once via
(nearest-swatch mapping) and the old keys are now simply ignored as unknown. There is no custom-color wheel and no explicit-hex escape hatch; changing what a pick looks like means editing

entries.note: the canonical 10-pick role table + per-role resolvers; leaf module importable by theme tokens, objects, and stage editor alike
CanvasHue/CanvasColor id vocabulary (no hexes) + isCanvasColor guard
OBJECT_TYPE_DEFAULTS (geometry/shape/label per type) + FIRST_USE_COLORS + colorKindForType — the reducer-readable defaults vocabulary the registry stamps onto defs
ObjectDef v2 + ConnectorDef types, OBJECT_DEFS registry, objectDefFor/objectDefForType dispatch
text-slot preset library (center / below / inset-body / title-chip / rect) + resolveTextSlot — renderer and editor both consume it
per-silhouette inscribed text rects used by the center slot
pure React-free outline builders, OutlineSpec consts + dispatch, getConnectionAnchors, outlineContainsPoint — connector-relevant outline math lives here
Shapes-panel ARRANGEMENT only; entry labels/keywords derive from each def's catalog metadata
shared object button chrome, ObjectSlotText, resolveObjectRoleColors (color role resolution)
sectionDef — title-chip slot, section color role, corner handles, descendants drag capture
stickyDef (inset-body slot, sticky color role) + markdown.tsx simple-markdown renderer (D18)
connectorDef (ConnectorDef, D19) + connector style constants; deliberately not in OBJECT_DEFS
shape-def.ts (ShapeDef) + base.tsx (shapeObjectDef adapter) + basic|flowchart|icon|misc one-file shape defs
cascade only (resolveConnectionCascade); consumes objects/geometry.ts for connector-relevant outline math
zoom-gated FigJam-style anchor dots at the def-derived anchor positions (ANCHOR_DOTS_MIN_ZOOM = 0.5)
THE one 10-pick color picker, identical for every kind
in-place text editor (use-text-editing.ts + TextEditingOverlay.tsx) — positioned/styled by the def's text slot; connector labels at the routed midpoint

text.insert: All behavior lives in ONE new file — a ShapeDef. The remaining touches are one-line entries in closed vocabularies and registries, each guided by a compile error or a test failure (the old duplication — catalog labels, four parallel default switches, the routing-layer
switch, per-shape editor placement — is gone; those all derive from the def now). Here is a real, complete, shipping def to copy — the hexagon:
Note what the file does NOT contain: no toolbar, no handles, no defaults, no text slot (
is the omission default), no editor code —
stamps all of that. The registration lines, quoted exactly as they exist for hexagon today:
Optionally, a true outline (skip both steps and the shape ships with correct bbox anchors —
): add a points builder + exported spec const to
, and reference the SAME const from the def's
field. Two tests police the whole recipe:
asserts the def's outline is identity-equal to the dispatch table's spec, and
asserts the def's stamped defaults match the reducer's row. Run
and the compiler/tests walk you through anything missed.

props.title: Shape vocabulary is closed and typed, not freeform SVG

text.insert: Extending the shape vocabulary means adding a value to

and touching the fixed registration sites above — not authoring an arbitrary SVG path, a runtime-registered shape class, or a plugin. There is no registration API, no user-supplied path string field, and no mechanism for a canvas document to define a shape the engine doesn't already know how to validate and render. An agent (or a human) constructing a shape only ever writes valid JSON against the schema; the def registry is compile-time, closed, and fully enumerated in
For the schema and reducer these shapes are validated and mutated through, see
. For the A*/anchor routing pipeline that consumes the outline/anchor contract, see
. For the per-shape enumeration with computed anchor positions, see
(note: its per-type prose predates the overhaul in places — this doc wins on the def/text/color contract). For how the shape set maps onto FigJam's own picker, see
